sentences.text: My name is it Jan Karski.
I was born in Lodz, Lodz in 1914.
My family, my parents, by American standards were rather poor.
My father had a little saddle wear factory on the lower level artisanship, producing saddles, women's bags, leather goods.
My mother, as most of the women at the time of course, had no formal education.
My mother was very, very Catholic.
But for some reason, she was tolerant.
Which when I became old-- an old man, I wondered.
Because she was a Catholic.
And she was fanatically devoted to the dictator in Poland, Pilsudski, who was on some occasions, in trouble with the church.
And she should know about it.
Somehow she combined it.
She was tolerant towards the Jews.
I was very Catholic as a child.
I entered the Catholic organization [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, so there were such cases.
In our house, apartment house, [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember.
In the yard by the [INAUDIBLE], the Jews would build sort of a hut out of, you know, wood, and then with branches.
And they prayed.
They have a name for it.
I forgot.
[NON-ENGLISH]
[NON-ENGLISH] Yes.
[NON-ENGLISH], yes.
Well, so, some boys, children, who my mother called bad boys, amused themselves.
They would sneak, you know, and then would throw over the roof dead rats.
And my mother did not like it.
So she said, well, if you are such a good boy and a Catholic, watch that they don't do it.
If somebody comes, don't fight.
Only shout, mama!
Mama!
I will take care of them.
And I did watch once or twice.
But nobody came.
So there was no danger.
My mother was very tolerant.
She did have influence.
I loved her very much.
I was telling her all my sins, all my bad things.
[INAUDIBLE] She was sympathetic towards the Jews.
My-- the name of my father was Stefan Kozielewski.
My mother, Walentina Kozielewski.
Oh yes.
We were eight, seven boys, one girl.
Two boys died at early age.
In Poland, as you probably know after the First World War, for many years there was hunger.
The situation was very bad.
One, as far as I remember, died of tuberculosis, which was very widely spread.
The other one, I don't remember.
And then, so then, we were left six.
All of them died, of course.
Now, I am alone.
I have no family now.
My sister, I think, she died at the end of the war.
My other brothers, two brothers emigrated just after the war.
Some went to South America.
I don't even know where they went.
The oldest brother came to the United States and died in the United States.
Yes.
All of them finish for this called gymnasium, gymnasium, which is high school by American standards.
I also finished gymnasium, Marshal Pilsudski Gymnasium, in Lodz in 1931.
Then I went to Lwow to do a university, studied law and diplomatic sciences and got MA 1935.
Then, of course, military service, which was compulsory in Poland.
I joined the [INAUDIBLE] artillery, which was the [INAUDIBLE] group.
I was primos, the best.
I got President of the Republic's honorary sword.
And with that sword, I went to the war.
The war found me in Auschwitz.
There was the base of my Fifth Division of the [INAUDIBLE] Artillery.
At the camp-- not [INAUDIBLE], but the camp Auschwitz, I know every house.
Well, so the war started precisely 1st of September, approximately 5 past 5:00 in the morning.
How I know so exactly?
Because the [INAUDIBLE], you know, getting up out of bed, it was at 5 o'clock, and then shaving, going to the toilet.
And then while we were shaving, some terrible noise.
The Germans dropped a half a ton of bomb.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I was not afraid of the bombs.
Because I believed very strongly if they killed me, I go to heavens.
God wants this way.
If you are killed in defense of your country, all your sins will be forgotten.
But those stupid horses-- I hate horses from that moment.
They have no soul.
They broke out of the stables.
You couldn't touch them.
[LAUGHS] You know, to the cannons, 75 kilometers-- millimeters, you know, cannons.
Crazy.
Chaos, confusion.
Within some two hours, we left the horses.
We left-- left the cannons.
And just withdrawing two days, two days, to the next position of concentration, wherever we would come.
Already, railroad bombed.
Already, you know, it is with some station, is at post office, is at [INAUDIBLE]?
Further two days, until precisely September 17, we reached Tarnopol.
This is the most eastern town, not far away from the Soviet boundary.
Now, chaos, confusion, hundreds of thousands of civilians.
We were withdrawing not in order, you know, everybody individually.
Everybody had to take care of his own needs, sleeping in some stables wherever we could.
I remember when I spent my money, first thing, I saw the [NON-ENGLISH].
I [INAUDIBLE] a fashionable [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE].
Because I planned to take pictures of German generals when they would parade in front of us in Berlin after we win the war.
[LAUGHS]
So the first thing, I saw the [INAUDIBLE] to some peasant.
He gave me some, you know, dried food, et cetera.
And some bag, so I was carrying the bag still having President's honorary sword.
So then when I spend-- you know, ate all this food, I entered some shop, again, peasant, you know and told him, listen, this is valuable.
There is President's inscription.
How much will you give for me?
I don't want money.
I want, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sausages, you know, et cetera, for food.
He looked at me and said, get out of this, you know, vulgar expression.
It will bring me trouble.
He realized the situation better than I, that it is lost, that everything is lost.
So I got out of that shop and dropped my sword, you know, [LAUGHS] in the field.
Because it was heavy.
And summer was very hot at that time.
And we came to Tarnopol.
And then in front, we saw some military, you know, some tanks coming.
And through megaphones, loudspeakers, some voice in Polish, bad Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are-- we came from the Soviet Union.
We are Soviet soldiers.
We came to help you and to fight together against the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the Germans.
Deposit your arms, whatever you have.
Officers to the right, and non-commissioned officers to the left.
They did not arrest civilians.
Your weapons will be given to you later after taking, you know, evidence.
Yes, we did it like sheep.
There was no possibility of any fighting.
Then, they took us to the railroad station which was not destroyed in Tarnopol.
They kept us some two days.
After two days, cattle the trains to the east.
Then I landed in some camp called [INAUDIBLE], not [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].
And I stayed there some four weeks.
It was in Russia.
And [INAUDIBLE] somewhere near Smolensk, I believe.
In that book published about me, there is a map.
And the map shows [INAUDIBLE], exactly where it is.
Well, it-- I must have been caught probably the end of September, until probably the late October.
From there, the non-commissioned officers stayed in the church, you know, former [INAUDIBLE], of course, reconstructed.
And the officers were separated.
And we stayed in some huts, primitive, or tents.

And then the commandeer announced publicly that non-- all non-commissioned officers who are of German descent or who were born on the territories incorporated into the Third Reich, if they so desire, please come to his office tomorrow if you want to be-- go to the German side.
Now, the German general staff committed itself-- also public.
It was public that all war prisoners of Ukrainian descent, Belarussian, or Russian, who wanted to go to the Soviet side of Poland, they would be allowed, men for men.
The same [INAUDIBLE].
Well, so I was very lucky.
Because at that time, I was born in Lodz.
At that time, Lodz was already incorporated into the Third Reich.
Not the-- as general government incorporated and even renamed as Litzmannstadt.
So [INAUDIBLE]-- so I had a chance.
I realized it.
It's a good point by me.
I would realize from the very beginning to get out of the Soviet hands.
At that time, we're not so much trusted.
But I considered Germany a civilized country, you know, and a higher level than Russia.
And I prefer to go to the German side.
And then, to make it short, we were cutting the woods to make a living.
You have to work in the Soviet Union.
So I exchanged my officer's boots which betrayed me as an officer with some soldier non-commissioned officer my size, you know.
Behind a tree, we changed the boots.
And I returned with the non-commissioned officers to their barracks, to their [INAUDIBLE].
And then next morning, go to the commandeer.
I want to be exchanged.
He ask me, you don't like it here?
I said, I love it here.
I would like to stay here.
But I-- just before the war, I married.
My wife is pregnant.
And those bad, bad, bad Germans, I am afraid, I want to help her.
I must join her.
This is why I want to go.
I am sure he realized that I was lying.
Simply, he was a good man.
This was for me a lesson.
There are-- there is no such thing as bad nations, good nations.
There are good people and bad people, not nations.
He was humane man.
And I went on the journey.
In [INAUDIBLE] another town on the bridge, they exchanged us, head for head.
The right-- on the right side, we were going to the German side.
Left, to the Soviet side.
Because that was the boundary.
And it was even amusing at that time.
Because we were exchanging insults and jokes, criticism.
Going to the German side, we were shouting, are you crazy?
Stay where you are.
[LAUGHS] Don't go to the Soviets, to the Bolsheviks.
You don't know what is waiting for you.
They were shouting at us.
You bet we're fascists, you pigs.
I said [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] you want to work for Germany?
You want to fight for Germany?
Ridiculous, it was ridiculous situation.
At this time, the Germans and the Russians had an agreement, right?
They cooperated, of course.
On September 17, the Red Army entered Poland.
And they divided Poland into Soviet Party, general government, semi-- semi-autonomous, but under Hans Frank.
He was Governor General.
And then he incorporated the territory with my city, Lodz [INAUDIBLE].
No.
Then I found myself in the-- in the camp.
Then after a few days, they announced that we are going to work on farms.
Again, the Germans took us.
Because we were non-commissioned soldiers.
You understand?
Yes.
They did not treat me as an officer.
Again, a cattle train going somewhere today northeast, in the direction of Radom.
And to make it short, I jumped from the train.
I escaped jumping from the train.
Cattle trains have narrow windows up.
So I made a patriotic speech to the soldiers in the train that, listen, I am an officer.
The war is not lost.
I have information.
There is powerful Polish army fighting in the mountains.
I have to join them.
Some, two men, have to raise me, you know, and throw me through the narrow window.
And so it was done.
They did it.
Well, it was in the middle of the night going as far away from the railroad as possible.
Yes, I found some village.
When you know, the light was coming, I picked up some poorest hut.
Because good houses, Germans could be there.
The poorest hut, I knocked the door.
Some peasant opened.
So I asked him, are you a Pole?
Yes, I am a Pole.
Do you believe in God?
Yes, everybody believes in God.
So I said, listen, God will punish you.
You will go to hell.
I need civilian clothes.
I am a soldier.
I am still fighting.
Give me anything.
You can keep everything I have here.
I must have civilian clothes.
And they did it.
They gave me, you know, milk.
This, I spent the night over there.
And next day in, you know, civilian clothes, in the direction of Warsaw.
So I remember this incident-- incident very well.
Because in that peasant hut, never before and never after, did I see so many fleas.
The bed was jumping [LAUGHS] with the fleas.
So when in the morning, I told the woman that everything was all right.
But those fleas, I couldn't sleep.
She scolded me.
She says, there is nothing strange.
They don't know you.
You are new here.
They don't attack us.
We live with them all the time.
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] And she resented that I complain about it.
They don't know it.
You are a foreigner here.
So this is why they were biting you.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
So then I went to Warsaw.
And in Warsaw, I started to look, you know, for friends, for my brother, et cetera.
No.
I was chosen as a courier.
Why?
Because before, ever since my high school, every vacations, I spend abroad in some other country.
A day, [INAUDIBLE] '18, '19, '20, to spend vacations in Poland, crazy.
So I went to Romania, to Germany, Switzerland, France, England.
I knew languages.
Then, I was young and strong.
Then, I was a sportsman, horseback riding, tennis.
I was a good skier.
This is why they chose me as a courier to France at that time.
And I knew Europe, because I was in so many countries.
And I will send at the end of December 1939 still to France where Polish government in exile resided recognized by the French government.
At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was [NON-ENGLISH], Union of the Armed Struggle.
Only by 1941, this organization was renamed as Home Army.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And it got an official status as a part of the Polish Armed Forces.
But it was in 1941.
In December 1939, still there was chaos, confusion.
There was no synchronization.
There were dozens of underground groups, et cetera.
Still, you know, it was the beginning, you know.
They were not organized yet.
And I was sent by one of such organizations.
Well, from Warsaw to France.
So, you mean, how did I go there, yes?
Yes.
No accident.
First, that organization gave me a guide.
The Mountaineer [INAUDIBLE] Is a spy in southern Poland.
I think-- I believe-- I-- I am sure he was paid, by the way.
He gave me, you know, skiing boots, both, you know, suit, et cetera, took care of it.
And he guided me through Slovakia.
How did we pass?
I was an excellent skier.
Slovakia, between Zakopane and the first Hungarian town, Kosciuszko, is very narrow, only this terrain.
The mountains, we passed on skis.
He was a good skier.
I was a good skier.
And he knew the ways to ski so high that Gestapo did not care every day to go over there, you know.
We dealt with great difficultly.
I reached Hungary.
The only thing, that since the boots were new, you know, I got blisters and Very bad.
It was very bad, so I had to rest in eventually Budapest some three days.
And I took to village, Kosciuszko.
In Kosciuszko, there was already an official Polish employee from the Polish Intelligence Army.
And receiving [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] escapees, you know.
Because hundreds of officers, soldiers, Jews, by the way, were going to Hungary.
As far as Jews, we are interested.
I'd like to repeat it.
At that time, which was 1939, 1940, I saw it.
Hungarian authorities, police, did not hurt the Jews.
They were protecting the Jews.
They did not deliver the Jews to Germans.
If they found somebody who-- without documents, but who would identify himself that he is a Jew, the only reaction was get lost.
Get lost.
Policeman didn't want to have anything, but did not arrest him.
Only later, Hungarians started [INAUDIBLE] police.
In 1941, means to the German authorities in Poland?
No, I didn't know it. 1941?
I didn't know.
Yes.
My main mission was that organization-- the name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- had a candidate for the delegate of the government mainly.
They were arguing the underground organizations have to be organized.
And there should be some highest authority who would represent the Prime Minister residing in Paris and become a head of the underground struggle.
They had a candidate.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
And my job was to present all the arguments and convince General Sikorksi that he should appoint him immediately as delegate of the government.
No, no.
No, in that little organization I was sent by?
No, I saw over there no more than six-- six individuals.
It was the beginning, I was unaware.
And everything was chaotic.

At that time only, when we speak about the Jews, keep in mind that the time is important factor. 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no sign whatsoever of what was coming two years, three years later.
The Jews only were-- I believe, by December that every Jew has to wear the Star of David.
Next, the Jews cannot publish.
The Jews cannot be MD's, cannot be doctors, cannot be lawyers.
The Jews cannot teach, cannot engage in public activities.
Then in smaller town, you know, ads appeared on the walls that Jews should live together in certain areas, but only smaller.
Warsaw was not yet ghetto even.
Warsaw ghetto was organized much later, I think. '40, or even-- the end of '40, only.
So at that time, at that time, yes, the Jews were discriminated.
But there was no sign of what will happen-- was to happen.
That so-called Holocaust was decided only-- I don't know you are aware.
February 1942-- a conference of the highest Nazi officials and the Gestapo in [INAUDIBLE], suburb of Berlin.
And their decision and [INAUDIBLE].
